h1Title: Bacterial Meningitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Bacterial meningitis is an acute, life-threatening infection of the meninges — the three layers of membrane surrounding the brain and spinal cord — caused by bacteria spreading haematogenously or directly from adjacent sites. The global incidence is approximately 2.5 million cases per year, with an overall case-fatality rate of 20–30% even with treatment, and neurological sequelae in 30–50% of survivors. The triad of fever, neck stiffness, and altered consciousness is present in only 44% of cases but, when present, demands emergency assessment within minutes. Bacterial meningitis is a medical emergency: each hour of delay in antibiotic administration increases mortality by approximately 7%. Empirical intravenous ceftriaxone should be given before lumbar puncture results are available if clinical suspicion is high.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 2.5 million cases per year globally (GBD 2019); incidence in high-income countries 1–2 per 100,000 per year, rising to 10–40 per 100,000 in the meningitis belt of sub-Saharan Africa
keyStats.demographics: Bimodal age distribution: highest incidence in infants under 12 months (25–50 per 100,000) and adults over 65; college-age students (15–25) at elevated risk for meningococcal disease
keyStats.avgAge: Neonates: group B Streptococcus and Listeria dominate. Children 1 month–5 years: Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis. Adults: S. pneumoniae predominates.
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 318,000 deaths per year from bacterial meningitis globally (GBD 2019); sub-Saharan Africa accounts for disproportionate mortality

definition: Bacterial meningitis (ICD-10: G00.9) is a purulent infection of the subarachnoid space and meninges caused by bacteria, producing an inflammatory response with potential for cerebral oedema, hydrocephalus, cerebrovascular complications, and systemic sepsis. The most common causative organisms in adults are Streptococcus pneumoniae (accounting for 50–70% of community-acquired cases in high-income countries) and Neisseria meningitidis (15–25%). In neonates, group B Streptococcus and Escherichia coli are dominant. In the elderly and immunocompromised, Listeria monocytogenes becomes a significant pathogen. Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) once accounted for the majority of paediatric cases but has fallen dramatically in countries with Hib vaccination programmes. Pathophysiology involves bacteraemia seeding the meninges, or direct extension from sinusitis, otitis media, or skull fracture. Bacteria in the cerebrospinal fluid activate cytokine cascades leading to meningeal inflammation, disruption of the blood-brain barrier, cerebral oedema, raised intracranial pressure, cortical injury, and vasculitis. Neurologists and infectious disease specialists lead management, with intensive care involvement for severe cases.

typesClassification.description: The most common and most severe form in adults, responsible for approximately 50–70% of bacterial meningitis in high-income countries. Case-fatality rates of 20–30% and neurological sequelae in up to 50% of survivors. Associated with pneumonia, otitis media, sinusitis, alcoholism, asplenia, and immunosuppression.

Responsible for 15–25% of cases; affects all ages but predominates in teenagers and young adults. The only form associated with a characteristic non-blanching petechial or purpuric rash (meningococcaemia). Serogroups A, B, C, W, and Y; group A causes meningitis belt epidemics in sub-Saharan Africa. More likely to present with fulminant septicaemia.

Early-onset (0–7 days): group B Streptococcus, Escherichia coli, Klebsiella, Listeria. Late-onset (7–28 days): GBS, Gram-negative enteric organisms. Associated with prematurity, maternal GBS colonisation, and prolonged rupture of membranes. High mortality (20–30%) and long-term neurodevelopmental sequelae in 25–50% of survivors.

Caused by Listeria monocytogenes; predominantly affects pregnant women, neonates, the elderly, and immunocompromised individuals. Important because Listeria is intrinsically resistant to third-generation cephalosporins — ampicillin must be added empirically whenever Listeria is a concern.

Caused by hospital-acquired organisms — Staphylococcus aureus, coagulase-negative staphylococci, Gram-negative bacilli including Pseudomonas — following neurosurgical procedures, CSF shunts, or penetrating head trauma. Requires different antibiotic coverage than community-acquired disease.

primarySymptoms: Severe, rapidly progressive headache — typically described as the worst headache of the patient's life — caused by meningeal inflammation, raised intracranial pressure, and cerebral oedema.
Neck stiffness (nuchal rigidity): pain and resistance to passive flexion of the neck caused by irritation of the cervical meninges; present in approximately 70% of adults with bacterial meningitis.
High fever (temperature over 38°C in 95% of cases), often with chills and rigors, from bacteraemia and cytokine release.
Altered consciousness ranging from confusion and lethargy to deep coma, reflecting cerebral oedema, raised intracranial pressure, and cortical injury.
Photophobia and phonophobia — sensitivity to light and sound from meningeal irritation — causing patients to prefer a dark, quiet room.

Non-blanching petechial or purpuric skin rash, present in approximately 40–60% of meningococcal meningitis cases; individual lesions 1–2 mm starting on the trunk and legs and potentially progressing to confluent purpura fulminans indicating DIC.
Nausea and vomiting, often projectile in character, from raised intracranial pressure.
Seizures, occurring in approximately 15–20% of bacterial meningitis cases, from cortical irritation, cerebral oedema, or cerebrovascular complications.
Focal neurological deficits (hemiparesis, aphasia, cranial nerve palsies) in approximately 20% of cases, indicating cerebrovascular complications or localized cerebral involvement.
In neonates and infants: non-specific presentation with irritability, high-pitched cry, poor feeding, bulging anterior fontanelle, seizures, and absent classical triad in many cases.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden severe headache worse than any previous headache, especially when accompanied by fever and neck discomfort — this combination demands urgent evaluation.
A spreading non-blanching rash starting as small red spots on the trunk or legs in any febrile child or young adult — this is a meningococcal emergency until proven otherwise.
Increasing confusion or lethargy in a febrile patient, including any change in behaviour, responsiveness, or orientation.
Stiff or painful neck when fever is present — attempt to touch chin to chest; resistance or pain during this manoeuvre in a febrile patient requires immediate assessment.
In infants: bulging fontanelle, inconsolable high-pitched crying, and reduced eye contact in a febrile baby.
emergencySigns: Non-blanching purpuric rash with fever — call emergency services immediately; this pattern is meningococcaemia and can be fatal within hours without parenteral antibiotics.
Rapidly deteriorating consciousness (GCS falling by 2 or more points over 1–2 hours) indicating cerebral herniation risk — requires urgent CT and intensive-care admission.
New focal neurological deficit with fever — cranial nerve palsy, hemiplegia, or aphasia suggests cerebrovascular complication or severe cerebral oedema.

Signs of raised intracranial pressure with papilloedema, Cushing's triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respiration) or impaired upward gaze — do not perform lumbar puncture without CT; IV antibiotics must not be delayed.
Septic shock with hypotension, tachycardia, and peripheral shut-down in a patient with meningeal signs — requires immediate resuscitation in parallel with antibiotic administration.

causes.description: The leading cause of bacterial meningitis in adults (50–70% of community-acquired cases). Typically arises from bacteraemia complicating pneumococcal pneumonia or direct spread from otitis media, sinusitis, or mastoiditis. Risk is highest in asplenic, immunocompromised, and elderly individuals. Penicillin-resistant strains are globally prevalent, requiring empirical third-generation cephalosporin plus vancomycin in many regions.

The most feared cause in teenagers and young adults because of its rapid progression from health to death within 24 hours. Spread by respiratory droplet from asymptomatic nasopharyngeal carriers. Meningococcaemia produces endotoxin-driven DIC and purpura fulminans. Five serogroups (A, B, C, W, Y) cause disease; vaccination is available against all five.

Once the most common cause of paediatric bacterial meningitis; now rare in countries with high Hib vaccination coverage (over 95% decline). Still an important cause where Hib vaccination is incomplete, and can cause disease in unvaccinated adults with asplenia or skull base defects.

A foodborne pathogen (unpasteurised dairy, deli meats, smoked fish) that penetrates the blood-brain barrier via intracellular trafficking mechanisms. Predominantly affects pregnant women, neonates, people over 60, and those receiving immunosuppressive therapy. Critical to recognise because standard cephalosporin empirical regimens are inactive against Listeria — ampicillin must be added.

The leading cause of neonatal meningitis in high-income countries. Vertically transmitted from maternal vaginal colonisation during delivery or ascending after rupture of membranes. Maternal intrapartum chemoprophylaxis (IV penicillin) has reduced early-onset GBS disease by over 80% in countries with universal screening.

causes.cause: Gram-negative enteric bacilli (E. coli, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas)
causes.description: Cause meningitis in neonates, postneurosurgical patients, and critically ill adults on intensive care units. Increasingly resistant to broad-spectrum antibiotics; carbapenem-resistant organisms present a major management challenge. Pseudomonas aeruginosa typically requires anti-pseudomonal beta-lactams (cefepime, meropenem).

riskFactors.factor: Age under 5 years or over 65 years
riskFactors.description: The very young have immature immune responses and absent bactericidal antibody; the elderly have waning immunity and higher prevalence of comorbidities. Both groups have the highest case-fatality rates.

riskFactors.factor: Asplenia or functional asplenia (sickle cell, post-splenectomy)
riskFactors.description: The spleen clears encapsulated bacteria from the bloodstream. Loss of splenic function produces lifelong risk of overwhelming infection by pneumococcus, Hib, and meningococcus (OPSI syndrome).

Deficient cellular and humoral immunity allows bacteraemia to establish meningitis. HIV-positive individuals have approximately 5× higher bacterial meningitis risk. Complement deficiencies are specifically associated with recurrent meningococcal disease.

riskFactors.factor: Living in close quarters (university halls, military barracks)
riskFactors.description: Meningococcal disease is transmitted by respiratory droplets from asymptomatic carriers. Dormitory living increases transmission 2–4-fold.

Non-vaccination against pneumococcus (PCV13, PPSV23), Hib, and meningococcus (MenACWY, MenB) removes the most effective prevention. Mass vaccination campaigns in the African meningitis belt have reduced meningococcal A meningitis by over 99% in vaccinated populations.

riskFactors.factor: Skull base defect, CSF leak, or neurosurgical procedure
riskFactors.description: Direct anatomical connection between nasopharynx and subarachnoid space allows bacterial seeding. Recurrent pneumococcal meningitis should prompt search for a skull base defect.

Alcohol impairs phagocytic function and opsonisation, increases risk of aspiration, and is associated with Listeria and pneumococcal bacteraemia. Cirrhosis carries 4–5× higher risk of invasive pneumococcal infection.

affectedDemographics: Infants under 12 months bear the highest incidence globally — approximately 25–50 per 100,000 per year in high-income countries before widespread vaccination.

Sub-Saharan Africa's meningitis belt (Niger, Nigeria, Mali, Burkina Faso) has historically seen epidemic meningococcal meningitis with incidence exceeding 1,000 per 100,000 during outbreaks; MenA vaccination has dramatically reduced this.
College students aged 18–25 are at specific risk for meningococcal disease from serogroup B and C strains due to dormitory living and social mixing.
Older adults over 65 have rising incidence of pneumococcal meningitis and the highest case-fatality rates in high-income countries.
Immunocompromised individuals — including those on anti-TNF therapy, chemotherapy, and long-term corticosteroids — represent an increasingly recognised high-risk group in clinical practice.
diagnosisOverview: Bacterial meningitis is a clinical and laboratory emergency: diagnosis and treatment proceed simultaneously. Classic clinical presentation with fever, neck stiffness, and altered consciousness should prompt immediate blood cultures and IV antibiotics before any delays for imaging or lumbar puncture. The full diagnostic workup proceeds as follows: two sets of blood cultures, then head CT if focal neurological signs, papilloedema, or GCS below 12 are present; if CT is clear, lumbar puncture (LP) is performed. LP findings in bacterial meningitis show turbid CSF with raised opening pressure (above 20 cmH2O), very high white cell count (typically 1,000–10,000 cells/µL, predominantly neutrophils), raised protein (above 1 g/L), very low glucose (CSF:plasma glucose ratio below 0.4), and positive Gram stain in 60–80%. Bacterial cultures confirm the organism in 70–85% of cases if antibiotics have not been given; sensitivity falls to 50–60% if antibiotics precede LP. PCR for S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis, Hib, Listeria, and GBS on CSF achieves 95% sensitivity and is not affected by antibiotic pre-treatment. Blood and CSF procalcitonin, lactate, and C-reactive protein support the diagnosis and help differentiate bacterial from viral meningitis. Serum procalcitonin above 2 ng/mL has a sensitivity of 96% and specificity of 89% for bacterial over viral meningitis (meta-analysis, Limaye et al. 2019). CT head is performed to identify complications: hydrocephalus, cerebral oedema, subdural empyema, venous sinus thrombosis, or cerebral abscess.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative organism and antibiotic sensitivities; positive in 50–75% of bacterial meningitis cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw from two different venous sites using sterile technique. Bottles incubated in the laboratory for up to five days. Preliminary results in 24–48 hours; full identification and sensitivity in 48–72 hours. Drawn before antibiotics when possible but should NEVER delay antibiotic administration.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard diagnostic test; measures opening pressure, cell count, protein, glucose, performs Gram stain, culture, and PCR to confirm bacterial meningitis and identify organism
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient lies curled on their side or sits hunched forward. Local anaesthetic is applied to the lower back. A thin needle is inserted between L4 and L5 vertebrae. CSF drips into sterile tubes — approximately 10 mL collected. The procedure takes 15–20 minutes. A brief post-procedural headache may occur and is managed by lying flat and drinking fluids.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects bacterial, viral, and fungal pathogens simultaneously in CSF; not inhibited by prior antibiotic treatment; results in 1–2 hours
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sent on a 1–3 mL CSF sample obtained at lumbar puncture. The automated PCR panel tests for S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis, Hib, Listeria, E. coli K1, GBS, HSV, and several other organisms. Available in most tertiary centres.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies contraindications to lumbar puncture (cerebral herniation risk), complications of meningitis (hydrocephalus, subdural empyema, abscess, venous sinus thrombosis), and alternative diagnoses
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard CT scanner, approximately 15 minutes. Contrast dye injected via IV; minimal risk of allergic reaction in patients without known contrast allergy. Performed before LP when focal deficits, papilloedema, or reduced consciousness are present.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Biomarkers that support bacterial vs. viral meningitis differentiation; procalcitonin above 2 ng/mL is highly suggestive of bacterial infection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw with same-day result. CRP may already be elevated by 6–12 hours; procalcitonin peaks at 12–24 hours. Not diagnostic in isolation but increases or decreases pre-test probability.

diagnosticTests.test: Meningococcal and pneumococcal urinary antigen tests
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects bacterial antigens in urine within hours without requiring LP; particularly useful for early or culture-negative cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A urine sample collected at any time. The lateral-flow cassette result is available in 15 minutes. Specificity for both pneumococcus and meningococcus is approximately 90%; sensitivity somewhat lower.

treatmentOverview: Bacterial meningitis requires immediate empirical antibiotic therapy — any delay in antibiotics increases mortality. The IDSA guidelines (2004, updated 2017) recommend starting empirical IV ceftriaxone 2 g every 12 hours (or cefotaxime 2 g every 6 hours) within minutes of clinical suspicion, without waiting for LP results if LP cannot be done rapidly. Add IV ampicillin 2 g every 4 hours whenever Listeria is possible (age over 50, pregnant, immunocompromised). Add IV vancomycin where penicillin- or cephalosporin-resistant pneumococcus is prevalent (MIC to penicillin above 0.1 mg/L), until sensitivity results return. Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV every 6 hours for 4 days, given 15–30 minutes before or with the first antibiotic dose, is recommended in adults with pneumococcal meningitis, where it reduces mortality from 34% to 14% and neurological sequelae from 25% to 11% (de Gans and van de Beek, NEJM 2002). Dexamethasone is less clearly beneficial for non-pneumococcal bacterial meningitis in adults and in resource-limited settings. Supportive care includes IV fluid resuscitation for shock (while avoiding hyperhydration that worsens cerebral oedema), seizure management with IV lorazepam and phenytoin/levetiracetam, intracranial pressure management with head elevation to 30 degrees, and avoidance of hypoxia. Prolonged IV antibiotics — 10–14 days for pneumococcal disease, 7 days for meningococcal, up to 21 days for Listeria or Gram-negative enteric organisms — complete the treatment course. Chemoprophylaxis with rifampicin (or single-dose ciprofloxacin) for close contacts of meningococcal meningitis cases is given within 24 hours of notification.

medicalTreatments.name: Ceftriaxone (2 g IV every 12 hours, adult)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line empirical antibiotic covering pneumococcus, meningococcus, Hib, and most Gram-negative organisms. Achieves bactericidal CSF concentrations. Continued for 10–14 days for pneumococcal disease, 7 days for meningococcal, pending culture sensitivity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mortality from untreated bacterial meningitis (near 100%) to approximately 20–30%; outcomes depend heavily on speed of initiation

medicalTreatments.name: Ampicillin (2 g IV every 4 hours, adult)
medicalTreatments.description: Added empirically when Listeria monocytogenes is a concern — in patients over 50, pregnant, immunocompromised. Cephalosporins are inactive against Listeria. Continued for 21 days for confirmed Listeria meningitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Without ampicillin coverage, Listeria meningitis mortality approaches 30%; with appropriate coverage, case-fatality falls to 15–20%

medicalTreatments.name: Dexamethasone (0.15 mg/kg IV every 6 hours for 4 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-inflammatory adjunct started 15–30 minutes before or with the first antibiotic dose. Reduces blood-brain barrier disruption, CSF cytokine storm, and cerebral oedema. Reduces mortality in adults with pneumococcal meningitis from 34% to 14% (de Gans and van de Beek, NEJM 2002 RCT). Should be discontinued if S. pneumoniae is not confirmed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NNT ≈ 5 for one additional survivor in pneumococcal meningitis; reduces unfavourable outcomes from 25% to 11%

medicalTreatments.name: Vancomycin (15–20 mg/kg IV every 8–12 hours)
medicalTreatments.description: Added empirically in settings with high prevalence of penicillin- or cephalosporin-resistant pneumococcus. Discontinued once sensitivity results confirm susceptibility to ceftriaxone. Requires therapeutic drug monitoring (target trough 15–20 mg/L).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents treatment failure in cephalosporin-resistant pneumococcal meningitis; mandatory empirical addition in regions where resistance prevalence exceeds 5%

medicalTreatments.name: Seizure management: lorazepam followed by levetiracetam or fosphenytoin
medicalTreatments.description: IV lorazepam (0.1 mg/kg) terminates acute seizures in bacterial meningitis. Maintenance anti-epileptic cover with levetiracetam or fosphenytoin reduces recurrent seizures that worsen cerebral oedema and prognosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Benzodiazepines terminate status epilepticus in approximately 80% of cases; subsequent anti-epileptic therapy prevents recurrence in 60–70% during the acute illness

medicalTreatments.name: Rifampicin prophylaxis for contacts (600 mg twice daily for 2 days, adults)
medicalTreatments.description: Given to household and intimate contacts of index meningococcal meningitis cases within 24 hours. Eradicates nasopharyngeal carriage and prevents secondary cases. Ciprofloxacin 500 mg single oral dose is an alternative.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Contact prophylaxis reduces secondary cases of meningococcal disease by more than 90% when given within 24 hours of notification

surgicalOptions.name: External ventricular drain (EVD)
surgicalOptions.description: Placed by neurosurgery to relieve obstructive hydrocephalus, which occurs in approximately 10–15% of bacterial meningitis cases from ventricular CSF outflow obstruction. Allows ICP monitoring and intermittent CSF drainage.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces ICP in nearly all patients with hydrocephalus; outcome depends on underlying brain injury from the infection

surgicalOptions.name: Drainage of subdural empyema or cerebral abscess
surgicalOptions.description: Neurosurgical craniotomy or image-guided aspiration for subdural collections or abscesses complicating bacterial meningitis. Indicated when collections exceed 1 cm or there is clinical deterioration.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Survival from meningitis-associated subdural empyema with timely neurosurgical drainage is approximately 70–80%

whySeeSpecialist: Bacterial meningitis requires urgent neurologist and infectious disease specialist review for organism identification, optimisation of antibiotic duration, management of complications (hydrocephalus, vasculitis, hearing loss), and rehabilitation planning. All cases should have audiological testing at discharge — sensorineural hearing loss is the most common neurological sequela.
doctorSelectionGuide: The initial treating team is typically emergency medicine, infectious disease, or neurology depending on the institution. Intensive care specialists manage haemodynamically unstable patients. After discharge, follow-up should include a neurologist for cognitive or focal neurological residua, an audiologist for hearing assessment, and a rehabilitation specialist if significant deficits persist.
hospitalCriteria: 24-hour access to LP, CT head, and CSF culture and PCR facilities
Ability to administer IV antibiotics within 30 minutes of suspected diagnosis
Intensive care unit for patients with GCS below 12, shock, or respiratory compromise
Neurosurgery for hydrocephalus, subdural empyema, or abscess complications
Audiology for pre-discharge and follow-up hearing assessment
keyFacilities: Emergency department with rapid diagnostic capability
Microbiology laboratory performing CSF culture, Gram stain, and PCR
Medical or paediatric intensive care unit
Audiological services for hearing loss assessment
preventionStrategies: Complete full vaccination schedules against Streptococcus pneumoniae (PCV13 at 2, 4, 6, and 12 months; PPSV23 for adults at risk), Hib, and Neisseria meningitidis (MenACWY, MenB).
Ensure meningococcal booster vaccination for college students, military recruits, and travellers to the African meningitis belt.
Screen pregnant women for Group B Streptococcus at 35–37 weeks and administer intrapartum IV penicillin to colonised women — reduces early-onset GBS neonatal disease by over 80%.
Administer rifampicin prophylaxis to all household and intimate contacts of confirmed meningococcal meningitis cases within 24 hours.
Minimise immunosuppression where possible; administer pneumococcal and meningococcal vaccines before planned immunosuppression or splenectomy.
lifestyleModifications: Complete the full prescribed IV antibiotic course — early discontinuation risks relapse.
Undergo audiological testing before discharge and again at 4–6 weeks; request cochlear implant referral if profound bilateral deafness is confirmed.
Attend follow-up cognitive and neuropsychological assessment — up to 30% of survivors have cognitive difficulties that benefit from structured rehabilitation.
Disclose the episode to vaccination services — unvaccinated survivors benefit from meningococcal and pneumococcal vaccination after recovery.
Monitor for fatigue, mood disturbance, and post-traumatic stress — common after critical illness; psychological support services should be accessed proactively.
Inform employers or schools of any residual neurological deficits — many require occupational therapy or educational support adjustments.

recommended: High-protein diet (1.2–1.5 g/kg/day) during recovery to support tissue repair and immune reconstitution
Adequate calorie intake — critical illness increases metabolic demand by 20–30%
Iron-rich foods or supplements to address anaemia common after prolonged infection and inflammation
Micronutrient-dense foods (leafy greens, nuts, legumes) for neurological recovery support
avoid: Alcohol during antibiotic treatment and acute recovery (hepatotoxicity, drug interactions)
High-sugar ultra-processed foods that promote inflammation during recovery
Uncooked or contaminated meats and unpasteurised dairy — particularly relevant for Listeria prevention in future
Excess sodium if corticosteroids are prolonged (fluid retention and blood pressure effects)

exerciseGuidelines: No exercise during the acute phase. Gradual return to light activity (walking) is appropriate once fever has resolved and systemic symptoms are improving, typically 2–4 weeks after hospital discharge. Patients with residual limb weakness, ataxia, or cognitive deficits should receive supervised physiotherapy and occupational therapy before return to exercise.
dailyManagement: Administer IV antibiotics at the prescribed schedule — doses must not be missed or delayed during the acute phase.
Monitor temperature, level of consciousness, and pupillary responses every 2–4 hours in the ICU setting during the first 48–72 hours.
Assess limb movement, speech, and seizure activity daily to detect neurological deterioration requiring CT or neurosurgical review.
Maintain head of bed at 30 degrees to reduce ICP while ensuring cerebral perfusion pressure is adequate.
Perform daily fluid balance to avoid both hypovolaemia (worsens cerebral perfusion) and excessive fluid administration (worsens cerebral oedema).
Begin oral or enteral feeding as soon as the patient is awake and protecting their airway to prevent malnutrition during prolonged illness.
prognosis: Bacterial meningitis carries a mortality rate of 20–30% overall in high-income countries; pneumococcal meningitis has the highest case-fatality at 20–30%, meningococcal meningitis 5–10% in immunised populations, and Listeria 15–20%. Survivors face significant burden: 30–50% have at least one neurological sequela at discharge, most commonly sensorineural hearing loss (15–25%), cognitive impairment (25–30%), motor deficits (5–10%), and epilepsy (10–15%). Five-year follow-up studies show that approximately 20% of survivors still experience significant cognitive or functional limitations. Prognosis is worse with advancing age, high initial bacterial load (CSF bacterial count above 10^7 CFU/mL), pneumococcal aetiology, delayed antibiotics, and absence of dexamethasone. In children, neurodevelopmental sequelae — including learning difficulties, behavioural problems, and hearing impairment — are detected in 30–50% at 5-year follow-up.
recoveryTimeline: Fever typically resolves within 5–7 days of appropriate antibiotics. Antibiotic course: 7 days for meningococcal, 10–14 days for pneumococcal and Hib, 21 days for Listeria or Gram-negative enteric meningitis. Hospital discharge typically at 10–21 days. Residual fatigue and cognitive difficulties may persist for 3–12 months. Hearing loss and motor deficits often do not fully resolve; assessment and rehabilitation begin before discharge.
complications: Sensorineural hearing loss — the most common sequela, occurring in 15–25% of bacterial meningitis survivors; caused by cochlear damage from toxins and inflammation; detected by audiometry before discharge.
Cognitive impairment and memory deficits — present in 25–30% of survivors; from cortical injury, hippocampal damage, and white-matter changes; neuropsychological testing guides rehabilitation.

Epilepsy — new seizure disorder developing in 10–15% of survivors; typically controlled with a single anti-epileptic drug; highest risk in those with cortical infarcts or cortical spreading depression during acute illness.

Cerebrovascular complications — ischaemic stroke, venous sinus thrombosis, and cortical vessel vasculitis occur in approximately 10–20%; detected on MRI; anticoagulation or antiplatelet therapy may be required.
Obstructive hydrocephalus — from arachnoid scarring and CSF flow obstruction; requires VP shunting in 1–5% of survivors who develop chronic hydrocephalus.
Limb ischaemia and amputations — from DIC and purpura fulminans in fulminant meningococcaemia; affects approximately 5% of severe meningococcal septicaemia cases.

supportResources.name: Meningitis Research Foundation
supportResources.description: UK and Ireland charity providing patient information, bereavement support, and research funding for all forms of meningitis.

supportResources.name: National Meningitis Association (NMA)
supportResources.description: US organization supporting survivors and families with educational resources, advocacy, and peer connections.

supportResources.name: CDC Bacterial Meningitis Overview
supportResources.description: Authoritative epidemiology data, vaccination schedules, and prophylaxis guidance from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

supportResources.name: NICE Clinical Knowledge Summary — Meningitis (bacterial)
supportResources.description: UK primary-care guidance on recognition, initial management, and follow-up of bacterial meningitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Viral Meningitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Viral meningitis (usually enteroviral) is self-limiting with a typical recovery of 7–10 days. CSF shows a lymphocytic pleocytosis (50–500 cells/µL), normal or mildly elevated protein, and normal glucose — unlike the polymorphonuclear pleocytosis, very high protein, and very low glucose of bacterial meningitis. Bacterial meningitis requires immediate IV antibiotics; viral meningitis in immunocompetent patients is managed with supportive care.

confusedWithConditions.name: Intracranial Abscess
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Brain abscess causes fever, headache, and focal neurological deficits but usually has a subacute course over days to weeks rather than the explosive hours-long progression of bacterial meningitis. CT or MRI shows a ring-enhancing lesion. LP is contraindicated when brain abscess is suspected. Treatment requires neurosurgical drainage plus prolonged antibiotics.

confusedWithConditions.name: Stroke
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Haemorrhagic or ischaemic stroke can cause sudden headache, focal deficits, and altered consciousness, but fever and neck stiffness are absent unless meningitis complicates the stroke. CT or MRI immediately distinguishes vascular from infectious causes. Bacterial meningitis does not produce the hyperdense vessel sign or infarct pattern seen in stroke.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculous Meningitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tuberculous meningitis develops over weeks to months, with subacute fever, night sweats, weight loss, and gradual cognitive decline — unlike the acute hours-to-days course of bacterial meningitis. CSF shows a lymphocytic pleocytosis with very high protein and low glucose, acid-fast bacilli on smear (positive in only 10–40%), and positive TB PCR or culture. Treatment requires a 9–12 month antituberculosis regimen.

relatedConditions.name: Meningococcal Meningitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Specific form of bacterial meningitis caused by Neisseria meningitidis; distinctive because of its petechial rash, rapid progression, and near-complete vaccine preventability.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculous Meningitis
relatedConditions.relevance: A subacute to chronic meningitis sharing the anatomical site but requiring entirely different — and prolonged — treatment with antituberculosis drugs.

relatedConditions.name: Viral Meningitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common form of meningitis overall; shares presentation but is typically self-limiting and does not require antibiotics.

relatedConditions.name: Epilepsy
relatedConditions.relevance: New-onset epilepsy develops in 10–15% of bacterial meningitis survivors from cortical scarring and is one of the most significant long-term sequelae.

relatedConditions.name: Cerebral Aneurysm
relatedConditions.relevance: Bacterial meningitis (especially Streptococcus viridans from endocarditis) can cause mycotic aneurysms in cerebral vessels; relevant in cases with subarachnoid haemorrhage pattern on imaging.

faqs.question: What is bacterial meningitis?
faqs.answer: Bacterial meningitis is a serious infection of the membranes surrounding the brain and spinal cord. Bacteria — most commonly Streptococcus pneumoniae or Neisseria meningitidis — spread through the bloodstream and infect the meningeal space, causing inflammation, brain swelling, and potentially death. It requires emergency antibiotic treatment.

faqs.question: What are the early signs of bacterial meningitis?
faqs.answer: Early signs include sudden severe headache, high fever, and neck stiffness that makes it painful to touch the chin to the chest. A spreading non-blanching rash is a specific warning sign for meningococcal disease. Nausea, sensitivity to light, and confusion often follow. These symptoms require emergency medical attention immediately.

faqs.question: How do you tell bacterial from viral meningitis?
faqs.answer: Bacterial meningitis is more severe, progresses faster, and shows specific CSF changes: very high white cell count with neutrophils, very low glucose, and high protein. Viral meningitis has a lymphocytic CSF with near-normal glucose. Procalcitonin above 2 ng/mL strongly favours bacterial. CSF culture and PCR confirm the cause.

faqs.question: What antibiotic treats bacterial meningitis?
faqs.answer: IV ceftriaxone 2 g every 12 hours is the standard first-line antibiotic. Ampicillin is added for patients over 50, pregnant women, or immunocompromised individuals to cover Listeria. Vancomycin is added where penicillin-resistant pneumococcus is common. Treatment is given IV and continues for 7–21 days depending on the organism.

faqs.question: Why is dexamethasone given in bacterial meningitis?
faqs.answer: Dexamethasone reduces the inflammatory response in the meninges, decreasing brain swelling and the release of harmful cytokines. In adults with pneumococcal meningitis it reduces mortality from 34% to 14% and cuts the rate of permanent hearing loss and neurological disability. It is given 15–30 minutes before the first antibiotic dose.

faqs.question: What is the meningitis rash?
faqs.answer: The meningitis rash is a non-blanching petechial or purpuric rash caused by meningococcal bacteria releasing toxins that damage small blood vessels. It begins as small red or purple spots (petechiae) that do not fade when pressed with a glass. Any spreading non-blanching rash with fever is a medical emergency requiring immediate ambulance call.

faqs.question: Can you survive bacterial meningitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. With prompt antibiotics, approximately 70–80% of patients survive bacterial meningitis. Outcomes are best when antibiotics are started within 30 minutes of diagnosis. However, 30–50% of survivors experience some neurological sequelae, most commonly hearing loss, cognitive impairment, or epilepsy. Survival has improved significantly since the introduction of dexamethasone adjuvant therapy.

faqs.question: How quickly does bacterial meningitis progress?
faqs.answer: Bacterial meningitis can progress from early symptoms to life-threatening illness within 24 hours. Meningococcal septicaemia is particularly rapid — patients can deteriorate from mild symptoms to DIC and shock in 4–6 hours. Each hour of delay in antibiotics increases mortality by approximately 7%.

faqs.question: Is bacterial meningitis contagious?
faqs.answer: Meningococcal meningitis spreads by respiratory droplet from asymptomatic carriers and close contacts. Pneumococcal meningitis is not typically spread from patient to patient. Close contacts of meningococcal cases require antibiotic prophylaxis (rifampicin or ciprofloxacin) within 24 hours of notification to prevent secondary cases.

faqs.question: Can bacterial meningitis cause permanent deafness?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sensorineural hearing loss is the most common sequela, affecting 15–25% of survivors from cochlear damage during the infection. All survivors should have audiological assessment before hospital discharge. Profound bilateral deafness may require cochlear implantation for speech and language development in children.

faqs.question: How is bacterial meningitis prevented?
faqs.answer: Vaccination is the most effective prevention. Vaccines against pneumococcus (PCV13), Hib, and meningococcus (MenACWY, MenB) prevent the majority of bacterial meningitis cases. Pregnant women are screened for Group B Streptococcus to prevent neonatal meningitis. Close contacts of meningococcal cases receive antibiotic prophylaxis.

faqs.question: What causes bacterial meningitis in adults?
faqs.answer: Streptococcus pneumoniae causes 50–70% of adult bacterial meningitis. Neisseria meningitidis accounts for 15–25%, predominantly in young adults. Listeria monocytogenes is important in adults over 50 and the immunocompromised. Gram-negative bacilli and Staphylococcus aureus cause healthcare-associated and post-neurosurgical meningitis.

faqs.question: Can bacterial meningitis cause brain damage?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bacterial meningitis can cause cortical infarcts, hippocampal damage, and white-matter injury. This leads to cognitive impairment in 25–30% of survivors, including memory problems, difficulty concentrating, and executive dysfunction. Children may develop learning difficulties and behavioural problems detectable at 5-year follow-up.

faqs.question: What is the meningitis belt?
faqs.answer: The meningitis belt is a geographic zone across sub-Saharan Africa from Senegal to Ethiopia where epidemic meningococcal meningitis has historically caused incidence rates exceeding 1,000 per 100,000 per year during outbreaks. Mass vaccination with MenA conjugate vaccine has reduced meningococcal A disease by over 99% in vaccinated populations in this region.

faqs.question: How long is recovery from bacterial meningitis?
faqs.answer: Acute hospital stay is typically 10–21 days. Fever resolves within 5–7 days of antibiotics. Full recovery of energy and cognitive function may take 3–12 months. Residual hearing loss, epilepsy, or motor deficits may be permanent and require long-term management. Rehabilitation begins before hospital discharge.
